Q1: I tried to be really structured with my studying because I knew I had limited resources and time. I'd dedicate about four hours most days, usually in the evenings after work and on weekends. I'd print out readings when I could afford it at the library and make detailed handwritten notes. Being 29 and coming back to education, I knew I had to be disciplined, so I created a weekly schedule and stuck to it as much as life allowed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what was happening with work. Some weeks I could stick to my regular evening study blocks, but other weeks when my job demanded more or bills were tight and I was stressed, I'd have to cram everything into the weekend. Deadlines definitely pushed me to adjust my schedule. I tried to stay consistent, but real life doesn't always cooperate when you're juggling everything.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if new assignments were posted or to download materials I needed for the week. Sometimes I'd log in to see announcements or check my grades after submitting something. I wasn't someone who browsed around much—I went in with a purpose, got what I needed, and then worked offline. With limited internet access at home, I had to be strategic about when and why I logged in.
Q4: Honestly, using the LMS never really became a daily habit for me. I'd log in maybe once or twice a week at the library where I had reliable internet. It was more of a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my routine. I think if I had better internet at home, I might have engaged with it more regularly, but my circumstances meant I developed workarounds.
Q5: The downloadable reading materials and assignment guidelines were the most useful to me. I could grab them, print them out or save them to my phone, and then work through them at my own pace at home. The written resources suited my learning style perfectly since I learn best by reading and taking notes. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students asked questions I was also wondering about.
Q6: I rarely watched the videos all the way through on the platform—I'd start them to see what they covered, then find alternative resources if needed. They took too much data and time to stream with my internet situation. I also didn't engage much with the peer interaction features beyond what was necessary because I was usually studying alone at odd hours when no one else was online.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. I didn't have the luxury of exploring extras just for interest—everything had to count toward my grade or my understanding. If I could see how something fit into the bigger picture of passing the course, I'd prioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. Required activities got my full attention because they affected my grade directly. When you're working hard just to stay enrolled and keep up with life responsibilities, you have to make tough choices about where to put your energy. I always intended to go back to optional materials but rarely did.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download or print materials and then spend hours working through them at home with my notebooks. I used textbooks from the library extensively and sometimes found free resources online that explained concepts better than the course materials. The LMS was really just my starting point—the real learning happened with my printed notes spread across my kitchen table.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd study for three or four hours straight, working through problems, re-reading chapters, and writing summaries, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd be deeply engaged with the material, making connections and testing myself, but since I wasn't logged in, it was invisible. My understanding was growing even when my login activity wasn't.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything available for the next few weeks, and then not return until I needed new materials or had to submit something. I'd work through those downloaded files multiple times, annotating them, creating my own study guides. One download might represent ten hours of actual study time for me.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when work got demanding or when I was worried about money. My attendance dropped during those times because I couldn't always make it to campus. The anxiety would creep up and I'd feel overwhelmed, but I've learned to push through those moments. The CBT sessions actually helped me develop strategies to manage those difficult periods better.
Q13: Work responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage the most. When I had extra shifts or was exhausted after long days, logging in felt like one more task I couldn't face. Confusion with some course concepts also slowed me down—I'd spend time trying to figure things out on my own rather than engaging online. But I always came back; I'm not someone who gives up easily.
Q14: Sometimes I'd log in and scroll through materials but my mind was elsewhere—worried about a bill or tired from work. I was physically looking at the screen but not really absorbing anything. Those sessions felt wasted, so I learned to recognize when I wasn't in the right headspace and would log off rather than pretend to study. Quality mattered more to me than just being present.
Q15: I postponed the discussion posts more than I should have because they felt less important than the main assignments. I also avoided some of the more challenging readings when I was already stressed, even knowing I'd need them eventually. It wasn't laziness—it was survival mode, picking battles when everything felt overwhelming. I'd always circle back, but sometimes later than ideal.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver for logging into the LMS. I'd work backward from due dates to plan my study schedule, and my login activity would spike in the days before something was due. Without deadlines, I might not have logged in for weeks because I was studying offline. They kept me accountable and connected to the course structure.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me to engage more when it was helpful and specific. Good feedback made me feel like my effort was recognized and showed me how to improve. When feedback was delayed or generic, I felt a bit disconnected and less motivated to put in the same effort next time. I really valued instructors who took time to give meaningful comments.
Q18: The course structure worked okay for me in terms of assignment deadlines being spread out, which helped with my scheduling. But the expectation of regular attendance was hard given my work situation. I appreciated having materials available online so I could catch up when I missed classes. A more flexible structure would have suited my circumstances better.
Q19: Having downloadable resources made engagement much easier for someone in my situation. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or consistent internet access. The heavy video content was challenging because of my connectivity issues. A course designed more around text-based, downloadable materials would have been ideal for my learning style and circumstances.
Q20: I think some students might be genuinely trying to engage but struggling to translate that into completed work. They could be confused about requirements, dealing with personal issues, or maybe perfectionists who keep revising but never submit. High activity might also mean they're lost and searching for help without finding it. Activity doesn't always equal productivity or understanding.
Q21: This actually describes me somewhat. My LMS activity was low, but I was studying extensively offline and completing my assignments. I think students who know how to learn independently might not need to be constantly logged in. We find our own resources, develop our own methods, and only use the platform when necessary. Low activity doesn't mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in, what they click on, and submission times. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone actually understood what they read, how long they spent thinking about concepts offline, or the personal struggles that affect their participation. They capture the surface but not the substance of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, asking myself questions, connecting new ideas to what I already know, and caring about understanding rather than just passing. It's about being mentally present and invested in learning, not just going through the motions. For me, engagement happens in my head and my notebooks, not necessarily on a screen.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite financial stress and work demands. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent at my kitchen table with printed notes, or how the CBT sessions helped me manage my anxiety and keep going. It wouldn't show my resilience or my determination to climb out of my circumstances through education. That's the real story of my engagement.
